coredata.dc:title: Efficient electrochemical synthesis of green fluorescent carbon dots for curcumin detection in common foods
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chuntong
Qi, Haiyan
Jing, Tao
Li, Jun
Zhao, Ming
Qiu, Lixin
Li, Qiuying
coredata.dc:description: Curcumin (Cur) is commonly employed as a food additive in the pharmaceutical and food industries. However, improper use of curcumin can lead to adverse effects on human health. In this research, we developed an electrochemical approach for the synthesis of green fluorescence nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (G-CDs) specifically designed for curcumin detection. The prepared G-CDs displayed strong fluorescent emissions at 540 nm, along with notable attributes such as excellent fluorescence stability against photobleaching, and resilience to changes in ionic strength. The G-CDs demonstrated selective recognition of curcumin, resulting in fluorescence quenching in a linear range of 0–140 μmol/L, with an impressive detection limit of 46.4nmol/L. Five distinct food samples were effectively detected for curcumin using the developed G-CDs nanoprobe, yielding satisfactory outcomes. This newly proposed approach offers simplicity, outstanding sensitivity, and selectivity, showcasing its promising capabilities as an alternative method for Cur detection without the need for specialized instruments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemical synthesis
N,S co-doped
Carbon dots
Curcumin detection
Foods analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimization of electrolytic synthesis conditions for G-CDs
Detection of curcumin in actual sample

The work has been generously supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO.52172092) and the Special Scientific Research Fund of Powerful Province Through Higher Education (NO.GJQSCYH-2022001).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM image of G-CDs; (B) Size distribution analysis of G-CDs; (C) XRD pattern of G-CDs; (D) FTIR spectrum of G-CDs.
XPS analysis of G-CDs(A) XPS survey scan; (B) Element distribution analysis; High-resolution of (C) C1s; (D) O1s; (E) N1s; (F) S2p.

Optical Characterization of G-CDs. (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum (Inset: fluorescence colorsin natural light and under excitation at 480 nm); (B) FTIR spectrumo; (C) CIE coordinates; (D) Spectrum of emission at various wavelengths of excitation.
(A) Salt tolerance; (B) Light stability; (C) pH sensitivity.
(A) Emission spectra of G-CDs upon addition of varyingconcentrations of curcumin; (B) Linear correlation between curcumin concentration and (F0-F)/F0.
(A) Selectivity analysis of G-CDstowards different substances; (B) Interference of various substances on the detection of curcumin by G-CDs.

(A) UV–vis spectrum of curcumin, excitation and emission spectra normalized to G-CDs; (B) UV–vis spectra of G-CDs, curcumin, and G-CDs+curcumin; (C) Fluorescence decay curves; (D) FT-IR spectra of G-CDs and G-CDs+curcumin.
CCK-8 assay of HeLa cells with diferent concentrations of G-CDs.
Electrochemical synthesis scheme for the preparation of green fluorescence nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon dots (G-CDs).
Fluorescence quantum yields under various experimental conditions.
Comparative performance of curcumin detection using various CDs.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery of curcumin in different food samples.

head.ce:title: Efficient electrochemical synthesis of green fluorescent carbon dots for curcumin detection in common foods

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, No. 42, Wenhua Street, Qiqihar 161006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp for State Market Regulation, Qiqihar University, No. 42, Wenhua Street, Qiqihar 161006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp for State Market Regulation, Qiqihar University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Technology Innovation Center of Industrial Hemp for State Market Regulation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Curcumin (Cur) is commonly employed as a food additive in the pharmaceutical and food industries. However, improper use of curcumin can lead to adverse effects on human health. In this research, we developed an electrochemical approach for the synthesis of green fluorescence nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (G-CDs) specifically designed for curcumin detection. The prepared G-CDs displayed strong fluorescent emissions at 540 nm, along with notable attributes such as excellent fluorescence stability against photobleaching, and resilience to changes in ionic strength. The G-CDs demonstrated selective recognition of curcumin, resulting in fluorescence quenching in a linear range of 0–140 μmol/L, with an impressive detection limit of 46.4nmol/L. Five distinct food samples were effectively detected for curcumin using the developed G-CDs nanoprobe, yielding satisfactory outcomes. This newly proposed approach offers simplicity, outstanding sensitivity, and selectivity, showcasing its promising capabilities as an alternative method for Cur detection without the need for specialized instruments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety problems have become a significant focus in society in recent times. Curcumin, an important flavonoid pigment derived from the rhizome of the perennial herb Curcuma longa, is found in various foods (Vichakshana et al., 2023). Curcumin is widely utilized in the pharmaceutical and food industries as a spice and edible pigment. It has a variety of pharmacological actions, such as antioxidant and anti-inflammatory, free radicals scavenging, neuroprotective, and anti-tumor activities (Reis et al., 2022; Song et al., 2022; Zahra et al., 2022). However, excessive consumption of curcumin can lead to pro-oxidative effect on DNA and cellular ATP depletion, causing necrosis (Alhusaini et al., 2021; Benameur et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2022). Moreover, the World Health Organization requires when using curcumin as a food additive the dosage stay below 3 mg/kg per day (Wieczfinska et al., 2021). Therefore, it is crucial to accurately determine the curcumin content in food products.

Electrochemical synthesis has emerged as a novel and unconventional approach for the production of carbon dots. This method offers advantages over traditional techniques, such as enhanced fluorescence quantum yield and reduced synthesis time (Lee et al., 2022). Moreover, the integration of electrochemical synthesis with advanced tools provides a controlled operating environment under normal pressure and temperature, ensuring convenience and safety during experimental operations. Consequently, electrochemical synthesis holds great promise for the preparation of carbon dots.

Previous studies have reported that most carbon dots (CDs) emit blue fluorescence and require ultraviolet excitation wavelengths (Krishnapriya et al., 2023; Lai et al., 2023; Magdy et al., 2023; Mancini et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023; Wei et al., 2020), which can potentially induce cell damage and necrosis. To mitigate these issues, it is crucial to develop long-wavelength CDs for various applications.

In this study, the green-emitting nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots (G-CDs) were synthesized through electrochemical synthesis using neutral red and 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid monohydrate as carbon precursor ( Scheme 1). We systematically optimized the reaction conditions, including material ratio, reaction time, and electrolyte concentration. The detection of curcumin using G-CDs was based on the principle of inner filter effect (IFE). Subsequently, G-CDs were employed for the quantification of curcumin in five commonly consumed food items, yielding satisfactory results. Furthermore, we conducted cytotoxicity experiments to assess the biocompatibility of G-CDs. Overall, the G-CDs provided accurate curcumin detection and enabled monitoring in complex food matrices.

2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid, L-Cysteine, L-Phenylalanine, L-Glycine, L-Arginine, L-Tryptophan, L-Threonine, L-Histidine, Acetaminophenol, Florfenicol, Erythromycin, Sulfamethazine, Curcumin, Chloramphenicol, Kanamycin, Apramycin Sulfate, Neomycin, and Clarithromycin were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Neutral red, Auramine O, Azodicarbonamide, Tartaric acid, Metronidazole, Artemisinin, and Isoniazid were provided by Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). EDTA, Urea, and Glucose were purchased from Tianjin Kaitong Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). AgNO3, CaCl2, KCl, NaCl, and NiSO4 were purchased from Tianjin Kemio Reagent Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). BaCl2, Cd(NO3)2, CoCl2, CuCl2, MgCl2, and SrCl2 were provided by Tianjin Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Sulfuric acid (98%) and hydrochloric acid (37%) were obtained from Beijing Chemical Factory (Beijing, China). Dialysis tubing with a molecular weight cutoffs of 500 Da was purchased from HuatongTianrui Biological Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Ultra-pure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm was used for all solutions, and all chemicals used were of analytical grade. Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) with high glucose was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Missouri, USA). Cell culture flasks were acquired from Corning Life Sciences (North Carolina, USA).

UV-Vis and FTIR spectra were recorded on a TU-1901 spectrophotometer (Beijing, China) and a NICOLET 380 spectroscopy (Massachusetts, USA). Fluorescence spectra and fluorescence lifetime were investigated by a Shimadzu RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan) and an FLS920 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK). HRTEM experiments were measured on a JEM-2100 F electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan). XPS analysis was performed using an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Massachusetts, USA). XRD patterns were obtained using a D8-FOCUS X-ray powder diffractometer (Massachusetts, USA). Cell cytotoxicity was evaluated using an AMR-100 microplate reader (Hangzhou, China).

The reference technique was used to determine the quantum yield of G-CDs, and quinine sulphate served as the reference material. Using the following formula, the quantum yield was calculated:
Y
G − CDs
=
Y
s
×
F
G − CDs
F
s
×
A
s
A
G − CDs
×
η
s
η
G − CDs
2
where YG-CDs represents the quantum yield, η represents the refractive index of the solvent, and G-CDs represents carbon dots, while s represents the standard substance. F indicates the fluorescence area, and A represents the absorbance.

G-CDs were synthesized via electrochemical synthesis using 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid and neutral red. 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid as the carbon, nitrogen and sulfur sources, neutral red as carbon and nitrogen sources. Briefly, 0.1015 g of 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid and 0.1008 g of neutral red were dissolved in a NaCl solution (0.1 mol/L) and sonicated until the solid was completely dissolved. Two platinum electrodes (size: 10*10 mm) were inserted into the beaker, and the reaction was carried out under a direct current (DC) voltage of 10 V for 20 min. The reaction was terminated and solution was naturally cooled to room temperature when it turned yellow.

Crude product was obtained by filtering the liquid through a 0.22 μm membrane. The solution was then transferred to dialysis bags with a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da and subjected to continuous dialysis for 24 h. The dialysis water was changed every 8 h to ensure complete dialysis. The solution within the dialysis bag was freeze-dried, G-CDs powder was obtained and stored at 4°C. The solution within the dialysis bag was freeze-dried, CDs powder was obtained and stored at 4°C, the powder weight was 0.052 g, the yield of CDs was 25.70%. The resulting carbon dots were named G-CDs (green-emitting carbon dots). In subsequent experiment, G-CDs was dissolved in water and the concentration of stock solution was 4.46 mg/L.

For the detection of curcumin, fluorescence surveys were carried out at room temperature. Stock solution of curcumin was treated at a concentration of 10 mmol/L. Various concentrations of curcumin were added to aqueous G-CDs solutions. The excitation wavelength of 480 nm was used in each experiment.

Five commonly consumed food samples including curry powder, ginger powder, jelly, instant noodles, and oatmeal were prepared for real sample assay. The samples were purchased from a local supermarket. Curry powder and ginger powder were in powder form and did not require grinding. The curcumin extraction solvent was prepared by dissolving curcumin in a mixture of methanol and ultrapure water at a volume ratio of 7:3. The solution was stored in a 500 mL bottle. Instant noodles and oatmeal samples were ground into powder using a mortar and pestle, while the jelly sample was hammered into a paste shape using a glass rod. The powder samples were placed in small beakers for further processing.

About 2.5 g of each sample was weighed into a beaker and added 20 mL solvent. Afterward, the solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 2 min to remove impurities, and pass through the 0.22 μm microporous membrane. Then the extracting solution was transferred to a 25 mL volumetric flask and diluted the filtrate to 25 mL. The pretreatment solution were stored at 4 °C.

2000 μL G-CDs (4.46 mg/L), 80 μL pretreatment sample solutions were added to a fluorescent cuvette, then 20 μL ultra-pure water was added to the cuvette, the final volume was 2100 µL. The spectra were measured at the excitation wavelength of 480 nm. The experiment was repeated three times in parallel to calculate the content of curcumin in each sample.

The cytotoxicity of G-CDs was evaluated on HeLa cells by measuring the reduction of CCK-8 relative to control cells incubated with an equal volume of PBS. HeLa cells in the logarithmic growth phase were seeded in a 96-well plate at a density of 103 cells/well in 100 μL of culture medium. Different concentrations of G-CDs solutions were added to different wells and further incubated for 24 h. Subsequently, the supernatant was removed, and 90 μL of free DMEM and 10 μL of CCK-8 were added to each well. The plate was incubated at 37 ℃ with 5% CO2 for 40 min. The wavelength at 480 nm was measured, and the assay was repeated at least three times. Cell viability (%) was calcluated as (ODexperimental well/ODcontrol well)×100.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of electrolytic synthesis conditions for G-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance the fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) of G-CDs, we investigated the optimal synthesis conditions by manipulating the electrolyte concentration, raw material ratio, and reaction time ( Table 1). Our results revealed that a reaction time of 20 mins, a mass ratio of 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid to neutral red of 1:1, and an electrolyte concentration of 0.1 mol/L were the appropriate conditions. Under these optimized conditions, G-CDs exhibited a fluorescence quantum yields of 31.43%.

HRTEM was used to examine the morphology and appearance of G-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1A, the shape of G-CDs was homogeneous and spherical, and the average particle size was 3.1936±0.8710 nm (Fig. 1B). Additionally, the spectrum of G-CDs showed a distinct peak near 24°, indicating the predominantly amorphous structure (Fig. 1C).

FTIR was used to characterize the surface chemical functional groups of G-CDs (Fig. 1D). An absorption peak at 3420 cm−1 was caused by stretching vibrations of the N-H or O-H bonds (Hamid et al., 2023; Sun et al., 2023). Peaks at 2970 cm−1 and 2920 cm−1 were attributed to C-H stretching vibrations. There was a absorption peak at 1630 cm−1 originated from the stretching vibration of CN bonds (Wu et al., 2023). The two peaks at 1500 cm−1 and 1440 cm−1 were likely associated with the benzene ring skeleton (Ren et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2022). Additionally, weak peaks at 1320 cm−1 and 1190 cm−1 were assigned to SO bonds and sulfur oxidestretching vibrations (Qi et al., 2022). The peaks observed at 1050 cm−1 and 631 cm−1, indicating both stretching and flexural vibrations of C-S bonds (Liu et al., 2023; Pei et al., 2023). The FTIR analysis confirmed the presence of -OH, -NH2, CC, CN, C-S, and SO functional groups on the surface of G-CDs, which are advantageous for sensing applications.

The structure and chemical composition were further characterized by XPS. The XPS survey spectrum of G-CDs ( Fig. 2A) exhibited significant peaks of C1s, S2p, O1s, and N1s at 284.80 eV, 167.97 eV, 531.20 eV, and 399.40 eV, respectively. The atomic fractions of C, O, N, and S were determined as 47.16%, 34.93%, 9.68%, and 8.24%, respectively (Fig. 2B), indicating successful nitrogen and sulfur doping in G-CDs. The C1s scanning (Fig. 2C) revealed three peaks at 283.98 eV, 285.38 eV, and 287.58 eV, owing to CC/C-C, C-S, and CN functionalities (Pei et al., 2023; Long et al., 2022; Khan et al., 2023). The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) displayed two characteristic peaks at 530.08 eV and 533.68 eV, attributed to C-O and SO bonds (Deng et al., 2021). The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2E) exhibited peaks at 398.44 eV and 400.18 eV, representing pyridine N and amino N moieties (Sun et al., 2023; Wu et al., 2023; Saengsrichan et al., 2022). The S2p spectrum (Fig. 2F) displayed two peaks at 167.17 eV and 168.18 eV, corresponding to C-S and SO bonds (Ibrayev et al., 2021; Qi et al., 2022). In general, G-CDs surfaces existed -OH, -NH2, CC, CN, C-S, and SO functional groups, which were in accordance with the FTIR data.

An investigation of the optical properties of G-CDs was carried out using UV-Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV-Vis spectrum ( Fig. 3A) revealed two main absorption peaks were at 260 nm and 350 nm, corresponding to the π→π* and n→π* electronic transitions of the surface functional groups CC and CO present in G-CDs (Liu et al., 2022; Long et al., 2022). Furthermore, the G-CDs solution appeared colorless under daylight, but exhibited bright green fluorescence under 480 nm light (insets in Fig. 3A), which was consistent with the CIE color coordinate (0.3122, 0.6538) (Fig. 3C). The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of G-CDs were determined to be 480 nm and 540 nm, respectively (Fig. 3B). Additionally, the intensity of G-CDs increased initially and then decreased within excitation wavelength range of 410–510 nm, while the positions of the emission spectra remained relatively unchanged (Fig. 3D), indicating an excitation wavelength independence of G-CDs. Notably, the half-peak width of G-CDs was 58 nm, narrower than that of most CDs, which could account for their excellent fluorescence properties.

To further explore the exceptional sensing performance of G-CDs, an excitation-emission 3D fluorescence matrix was measured, the measurement revealed a prominent center at (480, 538, 977) ( Fig. 4), suggesting that 480 nm was the ideal excitation wavelength, which was subsequently utilized for further experiments.

To evaluate the stability of the G-CDs, various factors including ion strength, ultraviolet lamp irradiation time at room temperature, and pH were investigated. The signal intensity ratio (F/F0) of G-CDs exhibited negligible changes with increasing NaCl concentration from 0 to 1.0 mol L−1, indicating excellent salt tolerance of G-CDs ( Fig. 5 A). The optical stability of G-CDs was evaluated by monitoring the fluorescence signal for 120 min under UV irradiation, and it was seen that the intensity was constant throughout the entire duration, demonstrating high photostability of G-CDs (Fig. 5B). Furthermore, G-CDs intensity was investigated across a selected pH range of 1–14 (Fig. 5 C). At pH=1, fluorescence was completely quenched, while the intensity of G-CDs progressively increased from pH 2–9 before declining. This phenomenon was owed to the protonation of surface carboxyl groups in an acidic environment, leading to a decrease in electrostatic repulsion and subsequent aggregation and precipitation, resulting in reduced fluorescence intensity (Wang et al., 2017). Remarkably, the intensity of G-CDs suggested minimal changes within the pH range of 6–13, indicating excellent pH stability of G-CDs, even under weakly acidic or weakly basic conditions. Thus, G-CDs exhibit favorable stability and are suitable for use in physiological environments.

To evaluate the sensitivity of G-CDs for curcumin detection, the emission spectra of G-CDs solution across existence and nonexistence of curcumin were discussed, with the excitation wavelength of 480 nm. After optimizing the assay conditions, the intensity of G-CDs exhibited a significant decrease upon the addition of curcumin in concentration (0 μmol/L∼140 μmol/L) ( Fig. 6 A). The quenching efficiency achieved a maximum of 69.31% when the curcumin concentration reached 140 μmol/L. A strong linear relationship was observed between the curcumin concentration and the quenching efficiency [(F0-F)/F0] of G-CDs. The linear equation was found to be: (F0-F)/F0=0.00522[Curcumin]+0.00235 (R2=0.99911) (Fig. 6B). The linear detection range was determined to be 0–140 μmol/L, with a low limit of detection (LOD) as low as 46.4 nmol/L (3δ/K). The steep slope of the curve indicated the high sensitivity of G-CDs for curcumin detection. Compared with other methods, our method exhibited a wide linear range and a low LOD ( Table 2). The reaction time of G-CDs was very short through electrochemical approach. Specifically, this approach enables the visualization of the synthesis process.

To examine the selectivity of G-CDs for curcumin detection, selective and interference experiments were conducted. Under the optimized experimental conditions, various substances, including common metal ions (Co2+, Ag+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Mg2+,K+,Na+,Sr2+,Ni2+, Cr(VI)), amino acids, small molecules (EDTA, Cysteine, Phenylalanine, Glycine, Arginine, Tartaric acid, Thiourea, Urea, Glucose, Tryptophan, Threonine, and Histidine), drug molecule (Paracetamol, Florfenicol, Erythromycin, Sulfamethazine, Metronidazole, Curcumin, Chloramphenicol, Kanamycin, Neomycin, Clarithromycin, Artemisinin, Isoniazid, Apramycin Sulfate, Auramine O and azodicarbonamide) and anion (CrO4 2-, MnO4 -, F-, Cl-, H2PO4 -, HPO4 2-, NO2 -, NO3 -) were investigated in the existence of G-CDs. All concentrations were 100 μmol/L. The results implied that the fluorescence quenching degree of curcumin on G-CDs was significantly higher than that of other substances, demonstrating the excellent selectivity of G-CDs for curcumin assay ( Fig. 7 A). Additionally, when other interference substances (1000 μmol/L) were mixed with curcumin (100 μmol/L), the impact of coexisting substances was negligible, indicating the anti-interference capability of G-CDs for curcumin detection (Fig. 7B). Overall, these results confirmed the specificity, sensitivity, anti-interference, and selectivity of G-CDs for curcumin detection.

To elucidate the detection mechanism, UV–vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy, FT-IR spectrum were employed. There was an overlap between excitation spectrum of G-CDs and absorption spectra of curcumin ( Fig. 8 A). Compared with curcumin alone, the UV absorption peak of G-CDs/curcumin was red-shifted 20 nm, which moved to the visible region (Fig. 8B). And the FT-IR of G-CDs/curcumin showed that the position of peaks at 2970 cm−1 and 2920 cm−1 changed and the peak intensity increased, three new peaks appeared at 1151 cm−1, 1115 cm−1 and 988 cm−1. Moreover, a characteristic absorption peak moved from 877 cm−1 to 835 cm−1 (Fig. 8D). The above-mentioned results confirmed the formation of new substances. After adding curcumin, the fluorescence lifetime remained unaffected (Fig. 8 C). Based on these analyses, the quenching mechanism between G-CDs and curcumin might be attributed to the synergism between inner filter effect and static quenching (Liu et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of curcumin in actual sample
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical applicability of our approach, we examined the presence of curcumin in five different types of food samples, namely curry powder, ginger powder, jelly, instant noodles, and oatmeal. These actual samples were obtained from a local supermarket, as previously mentioned.

Considering the maximum residue limits of curcumin in instant rice noodle products, jelly, and oatmeal, which are as low as 0.5 g/Kg, 0.01 g/Kg, and 0.03 g/Kg, respectively (These data are sourced from the National Standards of the People's Republic of China, GB 2760–2014). Our detection method with a limit of 46.4 nmol/L proved effective in detecting the concentration of curcumin in these actual samples. Following appropriate sample preprocessing, the determine concentration of curcumin in the five actual samples were 5.26 µmol/L (Curry powder), 4.30 µmol/L (Ginger powder), 0.07 µmol/L (Jelly), 3.42 µmol/L (Instant noodles) and 0.15 µmol/L (Oatmeal), respectively. After calculation, the actual amount of curcumin in samples were 0.51 g/Kg (Curry powder), 0.42 g/Kg (Ginger powder), 0.0068 g/Kg (Jelly), 0.3308 g/Kg (Instant noodles) and 0.0146 g/Kg (Oatmeal), respectively. To evaluate the performance of G-CDs, three different concentrations of curcumin (5 µmol/L, 10 µmol/L and 15 µmol/L) were added to the actual samples, resulting in the recovery rates ranging from 87.14% to 104.55% (R≤3.87%) ( Table 3). However, it was worth noting that ginger powder and curry powder are natural products without additional curcumin added. Therefore, all the analyzed actual samples met the required standards.

The favorable optical properties exhibited by G-CDs in the aforementioned experiments highlight their potential for applications in cellular imaging. To evaluate the feasibility of employing CDs for intracellular bioimaging, HeLa cells were utilized as a model system. The CCK-8 assay reagent was employed to assess the cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of G-CDs. Even when HeLa cells were exposed to concentrations of G-CDs ranging from 0 to 1000 μg/mL during a 24-hour incubation period, the cell viability remained above 87.35% ( Fig. 9). These results confirmed the low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility of G-CDs. Consequently, further investigations were conducted to explore the use of G-CDs as non-toxic labeling agents for in vitro cell imaging.

In summary, our G-CDs were synthesized through the electrochemical synthesis of neutral red and 2,5-diaminobenzenesulfonic acid and the fluorescence quantum yield was 31.43%. The G-CDs demonstrated selective recognition of curcumin, resulting in fluorescence quenching in a linear range of 0–140 μmol/L, with an impressive detection limit of 46.4nmol/L. The detection mechanism for curcumin using G-CDs was based on IFE. Furthermore, we successfully applied them to detect curcumin in five common food samples, obtaining satisfactory results. Additionally, G-CDs demonstrated successful cytotoxicity assessment, thereby highlighting their potential for biocompatibility. Overall, our study highlighted the potential of G-CDs as a reliable and instrument-free method for curcumin detection.

The work has been generously supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO.52172092) and the Special Scientific Research Fund of Powerful Province Through Higher Education (NO.GJQSCYH-2022001).

Tao Jing: Supervision, Project administration, Investigation. Jun Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Investigation. Chuntong Liu: Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Haiyan Qi: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Ming Zhao: Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Lixin Qiu: Validation, Software, Data curation. Qiuying Li: Writing – original draft, Software, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank National Natural Science Foundation of China and Heilongjiang Province Education for their continued support.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chuntong Liu is studying for a master's degree in College Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University. His research area is analysis technology.

Haiyan Qi obtained her Ph.D. degree from College of Chemistry, Jilin University in 2009. She joined Qiqihar University in 2011. She has been an associate professor since 2015. Her research area is spectral analysis.

Tao Jing obtained his Ph.D. degree from Environmental Science and Engineering Specialty, Harbin Institute of Technology in 2012. He has been working in Qiqihar University and has been a professor since 2013. His research areas are sensor and new catalytic material.

Jun Li obtained his Ph.D. degree in Engineering, Niigata University in 2012. He has been working in Qiqihar University and has been an associate professor since 2017. His research areas are natural products and drug synthesis.

Ming Zhao obtained his Ph.D. degree in Natural Science Research, Niigata University in 2007. He has been working in Qiqihar University and has been an associate professor since 2009. His research areas are isolation and identification of active natural compounds.
Lixin Qiu is studying for a master's degree in College Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University. Her research area is spectral analysis.
Qiuying Li is studying for a master's degree in College Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University. Her interest area is spectral analysis.
